title: Polymorphism
description: Decode dynamic dispatch, @Override, upcasting, downcasting, and runtime type checks — the polymorphism mechanics that make or break CSE110 output-prediction questions.

sections.content: 📚 Why Polymorphism is CSE110's Hardest Topic

Polymorphism questions are the **hardest output-prediction problems** in CSE110 exams because they require you to track two types simultaneously: the **declared (compile-time) type** and the **actual (runtime) type**. The declared type controls which methods are *accessible*. The actual type controls which method body *runs*. Confusing these two is the source of most exam mistakes.

rows: `Animal a = new Dog()` → only Animal methods accessible
JVM resolves at runtime via vtable

sections.label: 🚨 The Golden Rule of Polymorphism
sections.content: For **instance methods**: always ask 'what is the actual runtime type of this object?' — that's whose method body runs. For **static methods and fields**: always ask 'what is the declared type of the reference?' — that's what gets used. Exam questions are specifically designed to make you mix these up.

**Upcasting** is assigning a subclass reference to a superclass variable. It is always **safe and automatic** — no cast syntax needed. After upcasting, the reference can only access methods defined in the declared (superclass) type, even though the object itself is still the subclass.

sections.title: Upcasting and Dynamic Dispatch

sections.label: Why Upcast at All?
sections.content: Upcasting enables writing **generic code** that works with any subclass. Example: `void processAnimal(Animal a)` accepts Dog, Cat, Bird — all passed as Animal. Inside, `a.speak()` calls each animal's own version. This is the power of polymorphism.

**Downcasting** converts a superclass reference back to a subclass type. It requires **explicit cast syntax** `(SubClass) ref` and can cause a **`ClassCastException`** at runtime if the actual object is not of that subclass. Always use `instanceof` to check before downcasting.

sections.title: Safe Downcasting with instanceof

sections.content: 🔁 Polymorphism in Action — Method Dispatch Table

When tracing polymorphic output, build a mental table: for each method call, identify (1) the declared type to check accessibility, (2) the actual type to find the executing method body, and (3) whether it is an instance or static method.

sections.title: Full Dispatch Trace Example

sections.label: Fields Are NOT Polymorphic!
sections.content: Unlike methods, **instance fields** are resolved by the **declared type**, not the runtime type. If both parent and child have a field named `type`, accessing `v.type` where `v` is declared as `Vehicle` always gives the Vehicle field — even if the actual object is a Car. This is field shadowing, not overriding.

Polymorphism becomes even more powerful with abstract classes and interfaces (covered in Ch13). An abstract class forces every subclass to provide its own implementation of certain methods, guaranteeing that `shape.area()` always works — even without knowing whether `shape` is a Circle, Rectangle, or Triangle at compile time.

sections.title: Polymorphic Array — One Loop, Many Behaviors
Exercise 1: Classic Dynamic Dispatch Chain
sections.explanation: Step 1: `obj.m1()` — declared type P, actual type R. R overrides m1(). So R.m1() runs. Step 2: Inside R.m1(), `super.m1()` is called. `super` refers to Q. Q does NOT override m1(), so we go up to P.m1(). Step 3: P.m1() prints 'P.m1', then calls `m2()`. Here is the key: `this` inside P.m1() still refers to the original R object → dynamic dispatch for m2() → finds Q.m2() (R doesn't override m2, Q does). Prints 'Q.m2'. Step 4: P.m1() returns. Back in R.m1(): prints 'R.m1'. Final output: P.m1 / Q.m2 / R.m1.

sections.title: Exercise 2: Field Shadowing vs Method Override
sections.explanation: Line 1 `b.x`: `b` declared as Base → field access uses declared type → Base.x = 10. Line 2 `b.getX()`: instance method → dynamic dispatch → actual type is Derived → Derived.getX() returns Derived.x = 20. Line 3 `d.x`: `d` declared as Derived → field access uses declared type Derived → Derived.x = 20. Line 4 `d.getX()`: declared type Derived, actual type Derived → Derived.getX() = 20. Key lesson: fields use declared type, methods use actual type — same object, different rules.

sections.title: Exercise 3: Illegal Method Call on Upcast Reference
sections.error: Line 9: Compile-time error — method purr() is not defined in class Animal. The declared type of `a` is Animal, and Animal has no purr() method. Even though the actual object is a Cat, the compiler only checks the declared type for method accessibility.
sections.explanation: The declared type of the reference (Animal) acts as the 'menu' of what the compiler allows you to call. purr() is not on that menu. To access it, downcast to Cat using `(Cat) a` — but only after verifying with instanceof to avoid ClassCastException at runtime. This is a **compile-time** error.

sections.title: Exercise 4: ClassCastException at Runtime
sections.error: Line 16: Runtime error — ClassCastException. The actual type of `s` is Square, not Circle. Casting a Square to Circle is illegal at runtime even though both are subclasses of Shape. This compiles successfully because the compiler only checks that Square and Circle share a common ancestor (Shape). The JVM throws ClassCastException when it executes the cast.
sections.explanation: This is a **runtime** error (ClassCastException), NOT a compile-time error. The compiler allows the cast because Square and Circle are related through Shape — the compiler cannot always determine the actual type. The JVM checks the actual type at runtime and throws ClassCastException when they don't match. Always use instanceof before downcasting.

sections.title: Exercise 5: Polymorphic Constructor and toString() Override
sections.explanation: This is a subtle polymorphism-in-constructor trap. Step 1: `new Premium(1000, 200)` → calls Premium(1000, 200) → first line is `super(1000)` → Account constructor runs with balance=1000. Step 2: Inside Account constructor, `getBalance()` is called. THIS is where polymorphism applies even during construction — the actual type is already Premium, so Premium.getBalance() runs (not Account.getBalance()). At this point bonus is 0.0 (default, not yet set). Wait — balance=1000, bonus=0.0 → getBalance()=1000+0=1000.0. Hmm, but then after super() returns, `this.bonus=200` is set. Let me retrace: In Account constructor: balance=1000 (set by this.balance=b). getBalance() called — actual type Premium → returns balance+bonus = 1000+0.0 = 1000.0. Prints 'Account: 1000.0'. Back in Premium: this.bonus=200. Now getBalance()=1200.0. Prints 'Premium: 1200.0'. main: a.getBalance() → Premium → 1200.0. Output: Account: 1000.0 / Premium: 1200.0 / 1200.0.

sections.title: Exercise 6: Implement a Polymorphic Shape Calculator
sections.approach: Define Shape with abstract-style methods (return 0 as defaults). Override in each subclass. The processAll() method takes a Shape[] array and calls name(), area(), perimeter() on each — dynamic dispatch selects the right implementation. Heron's formula: s=(a+b+c)/2, area=sqrt(s*(s-a)*(s-b)*(s-c)).
sections.explanation: processAll() demonstrates runtime polymorphism: the Shape[] array holds objects of different actual types. The call `s.area()` dispatches to the correct subclass implementation at runtime via dynamic dispatch. Notice how the method signature stays `Shape` — this is the open/closed principle: we can add a new shape type without changing processAll(). Triangle(3,4,5): s=6, area=sqrt(6*3*2*1)=sqrt(36)=6.0. ✓

items: **Dynamic dispatch**: for instance methods, the method body that runs is determined by the **actual (runtime) type**, not the declared type
**Upcasting**: subclass → superclass reference; automatic, always safe; limits accessible methods to declared type
**Downcasting**: superclass → subclass reference; requires explicit cast `(SubClass)`; throws `ClassCastException` at runtime if actual type doesn't match
**`instanceof`**: always check before downcasting; returns `false` for `null`
**Fields are NOT polymorphic**: field access resolves by declared type — even when an overriding instance method would use the subclass version
**Static methods are NOT polymorphic**: resolved at compile time by declared type (method hiding, not overriding)

**Polymorphism in constructors**: even during object construction, instance method calls use dynamic dispatch — subclass methods can be called before subclass constructor has run, so subclass fields may be at their default values
**`@Override` annotation**: use always; causes compile error if the signature doesn't actually override a parent method, preventing silent bugs
**Compile-time vs runtime errors**: calling a method not in the declared type = compile error; bad downcast = `ClassCastException` at runtime
